focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The Bebop in fuel-save mode sounded different. With the engines down to a careful sip and half the lights cut, the ship stopped pretending to be dignified and let its old bones speak. Coolant ticked in the wall behind the galley. A vent fan worried the same dry bearing every seventh turn. The refrigerator gave a low irritated hum, quit, thought about it, and tried again.

Ed liked the stripped-down version of the ship. Fewer noises meant the hidden ones came forward.
Thin red dust still striped the floor by the couch where Spike had come in before dawn and stopped walking.

Data made noise too. Not to everybody. To Jet, a screen was a screen until it told him whether he could afford noodles. To Spike, a screen was something you glanced at for exits and trouble and then ignored on purpose. To Faye, a screen was debt, odds, a bill, a face, a lie. To Ed, the different systems all had their own little songs. Bounty archives were stiff and papery, all rustle and clack, like somebody riffling old cards with dry fingers. Port authority ledgers had a tinny beep to them, money-counting sounds. Hospital back-end systems lived in cold blue clicks. Obsolete syndicate freight code came in greasy, low, and red, the kind of sound that stuck to a wrench.

Tonight the songs were everywhere.

Ed had turned the common room floor into a nest. Two portable monitors from the engine room leaned against the couch. One stolen galley stool held a projector. Three hand terminals were spread open in a circle around Ed's bare feet. Thin cables ran under the table, over the arm of the couch, through the handle of a pot Jet had not found missing yet, and into the access panel by the main screen. Ein lay under the table with his nose on Ed's ankle, eyes mostly closed, ears too awake to co...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...as why it had not come with revival. When they thawed her, the hospital had needed a body, a signature, someplace to send the debt. They had not needed the years in the middle, and the system did not go looking for damage that did not interrupt billing. They woke the right woman. That had been enough for everybody except the woman.

Her hand rose toward her collarbone and stopped. That stupid little touch was the part she could not get past. If the other woman had been using the file years before Faye woke, then Faye had not learned the gesture from her in any ordinary way. Either it belonged to the lost version of Faye from before the ice, or habit lived somewhere memory could not reach, which was worse.

She turned to the certified log. Clinic intake. Debt flags. Transit checks. Temporary licenses. Nobody got rich in there. The woman who used her name had spent it on medicine, rooms, crossings, the long cheap paperwork of staying alive. Faye tried to hate her cleanly and couldn't. The theft stayed theft. Survival kept getting in the way.

She pulled her jacket aside and reached back to the smooth skin over her left shoulder blade through the fabric of her top. Nothing. No raised line, no seam to match Miriam's memory. The absence should have been relief. Instead it made the split wider. This body was hers. Those eleven years were not. The name sat between them anyway.

She put the papers back in order and held the top page flat with one hand. Faye Valentine, claimant confirmed. The proof was light enough to carry and mean enough to change the shape of tomorrow. Outside, the district went dark. Inside, the office kept humming around her. She did not cry. She sat with one hand on the printout and the other on the unmarked place on her back and let the vertigo stay ugly.
